questions.question: What is the adherence paradox, and why does it matter for people with mental health conditions?

options.text: People with mental health conditions find exercise easier than healthy people

Exercise is most beneficial for people with mental health symptoms, but mental health symptoms (fatigue, anhedonia, hopelessness, anxiety) make exercise hardest to start and maintain — this is a clinical barrier, not willpower failure
Mental health has no effect on exercise adherence
Only lazy people struggle with exercise adherence

questions.explanation: The adherence paradox: the people who need exercise most (those with depression, anxiety, trauma) face the greatest barriers to starting and sustaining it. Depression causes fatigue, anhedonia (inability to feel pleasure), and hopelessness — all directly undermining motivation. Anxiety makes public exercise threatening. Trauma makes interoceptive awareness unsafe. This isn't laziness — it's a neurobiological and psychological barrier requiring specific strategies, not willpower shaming.

questions.question: What is the principle of 'graded exposure' applied to exercise, and why does it work?

options.text: Start with maximum effort immediately to see results faster

Start ridiculously small (below your resistance threshold — e.g., putting on shoes, walking to the door) then gradually increase; each small success builds self-efficacy, which predicts long-term adherence
Graded exposure doesn't apply to exercise, only anxiety treatment
You should wait until you feel fully motivated before starting

questions.explanation: Graded exposure (from anxiety treatment) applies to exercise: start below your anxiety/resistance threshold with absurdly small steps. Example ladder: Week 1 put on exercise clothes daily (even if you don't exercise), Week 2 add 'walk to front door,' Week 3 add '5-min walk around block.' Each completion builds self-efficacy (belief you CAN do this), which is the strongest predictor of long-term adherence. Small successes matter more than physical dose early on.

questions.question: What does the lesson say about the relationship between motivation and action?

options.text: You must feel motivated before you can take action

Motivation follows action more often than action follows motivation — you start despite low motivation, notice the mood boost afterward, and let that become your motivation for next time (behavioral activation principle)
Motivation is the only thing that matters for exercise
Action and motivation are completely unrelated

questions.explanation: The lesson teaches: waiting to FEEL motivated before exercising is backwards — you'll almost never feel like it (especially with depression/anxiety). Instead: (1) start despite low motivation (use 2-minute rule or promise to stop after 5 min if unbearable), (2) notice the mood boost afterward (dopamine, endorphins, accomplishment), (3) let that post-exercise feeling become your motivation for next time. This is behavioral activation: action generates motivation, not vice versa.

questions.question: When is difficulty adhering to exercise a CLINICAL issue requiring professional treatment, not just a willpower issue?

options.text: Difficulty adhering to exercise is always just laziness

When experiencing severe anhedonia (no pleasure from anything), psychomotor retardation (moving through molasses), profound fatigue (sleeping 10-14 hrs still exhausted), or hopelessness — these require professional evaluation, not just discipline
Everyone can just willpower their way through clinical depression
Professional help is never needed for exercise adherence

questions.explanation: The lesson is clear: severe anhedonia, psychomotor retardation, profound fatigue (sleeping 10-14 hrs and still exhausted), and hopelessness are symptoms of clinical depression requiring professional treatment. Trying to 'discipline your way' through severe depression with exercise alone is like running a marathon with a broken leg. Seek professional evaluation — exercise can be part of your treatment plan once stabilized, but it's not sufficient as standalone intervention for severe symptoms.

questions.question: Identify your top 2-3 barriers to exercise right now (time, fatigue, self-consciousness, past failures, lack of enjoyment, pain, etc.). For each barrier, what's one small strategy from this lesson you could try (graded exposure, cognitive reappraisal, social support, habit stacking, environmental design)?
questions.explanation: This reflection applies the lesson's adherence strategies to your specific situation. Common barriers + strategies: Time → habit stacking (attach to existing routine), cognitive reappraisal ('I don't have time NOT to exercise'); Fatigue → start with 3-5 min and see how you feel, choose low-intensity (walking); Self-consciousness → home workouts or beginner-friendly settings; Past failures → reframe as data ('what didn't work teaches me what might'), graded exposure (start smaller this time). The goal is matching strategy to barrier.
